We grow vibrant partnerships Overview 06 Notice of Annual General Meeting 09 Profile of our new CEO Results at a glance Board of Directors Corporate profile Stanbic IBTC Group credit ratings Vision and values Standard Bank Group network Recognitions P04 P05 OVERVIEW Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC, Annual Report/Year ended 31 December 2025

Ordinary business: 1. To receive and consider the Report of the Directors and the Financial Statements for the year ended 31 December 2025 and the Auditors' Report thereon. 2. To declare a dividend. 3. To re-elect retiring Directors and to approve the appointment of a new Director. 4. To disclose the remuneration of the Managers of the Company. 5. To approve the appointment of New External Auditors and to authorise the Directors to fix the remuneration of the Auditors for the ensuing year and 6. To elect members of the Audit Committee. Special business: 7. To consider and if thought fit pass the following as an ordinary resolution:

"That the Non-Executive directors' annual fees for the year ending 31 December 2026 be and is hereby fixed at NGN804,525,000.00" 8. To consider, and, if thought fit, pass the following resolution as an ordinary resolution:

"That in compliance with the Rules of Nigeria Exchange Limited governing transactions with Related Parties or Interested Persons, the Company and its related entities (the Group) be and are hereby granted a General Mandate in respect of all recurrent transactions entered into with a related party or interested person provided such transactions are of a revenue or trading nature or are necessary for the Company's day-to-day operations (as specified in the General Mandate Circular sent to Shareholders along with the Annual Report). This mandate shall commence on the date on which this resolution is passed and shall continue to operate until the date on which the next Annual General Meeting of the Company is held in 2027." Notice of Annual General Meeting Chidi Okezie Company Secretary Dated this 24 day of April 2026 By order of the Board: Proxies Only members are entitled to be represented at the meeting. A member entitled to attend and vote may appoint one or more proxies to attend and vote instead of him or her. A proxy need not be a member of the company. For a proxy to be valid, it must be appropriately stamped (at the rate of NGN500) at the stamp duties office and deposited at the registered office of the company or the office of the Registrars (First Registrars & Investors Services Limited, 2 Abebe Village Iganmu Lagos) at least forty-eight (48) hours before the time fixed for the meeting. A blank proxy form is forwarded with the notice of meeting. Dividends If the dividend recommended by the Directors is approved at the Meeting, the bank accounts of shareholders with the appropriate e-dividend mandate, will be credited on Tuesday, 26 May 2026, to shareholders whose names appear on the Register of shareholders at the close of business on Monday, 04 May 2026. Closure of Register The Register of members was closed from Tuesday, 05 May 2026 to Tuesday, 12 May 2026. Unclaimed Dividends Several dividends remain unclaimed or are yet to be presented for payment or returned to the Registrars for revalidation. A schedule of members who are yet to claim their dividends will be circulated to shareholders along with the Annual Report and Financial Statements. Members affected are advised to write or call at the office of the Company's Registrars, First Registrars & Investors Services Limited, Plot 2, Abebe Village, Iganmu, Lagos during normal working hours. E-Dividend Notice is hereby given to all shareholders to open bank accounts in order to take advantage of the E-dividend payment platform. A detachable application form for e-dividend and e-bonus is attached to the Annual Report to enable all shareholders furnish particulars of their accounts to the Registrars (First Registrars & Investors Services Limited) as soon as possible. We request our shareholders to use the e-dividend payment portal that will serve as an on-line verification and communication medium for e-dividend mandate processing through the new E-Dividend Mandate Management System jointly introduced by the Central Bank of Nigeria, Securities and Exchange Commission, Nigeria Inter-Bank Settlement Systems PLC and the Institute of Capital Market Registrars. Rights of Shareholders to ask Questions Shareholders have a right to ask questions not only at the Meeting, but also in writing before the Meeting, and such questions must be submitted to the Company Secretary at the Head Office of the Company (Stanbic IBTC Towers, Walter Carrington Crescent, Victoria Island, Lagos) or by email to Chidi.Okezie@stanbicibtc.com or Oreoluwa.Esan@stanbicibtc.com, on or before Monday, 18 May 2026. Voting Voting shall be by show of hands. Voting by Interested Persons In line with the provisions of Rule 20.8 (h) Rules Governing Related Party Transaction of Nigerian Exchange Limited, interested persons have undertaken to ensure that their proxies, representatives or associates shall abstain from voting on resolution 8 above. Audit Committee In accordance with Section 404(6) of the Companies and Allied Matters Act 2020, any shareholder may nominate another shareholder for appointment to the Audit Committee. Such nomination shall be in writing and must reach the Company Secretary not less than twenty-one (21) days before the date of the Annual General Meeting. The Companies and Allied Matters Act 2020, stipulates that all members of the audit committee shall be financially literate, and at least one member shall be a member of a professional accounting body in Nigeria established by an Act of the National Assembly. Also, the Securities and Exchange Commission's Code of Corporate Governance has indicated that members of the Audit Committee should have basic financial literacy and should be able to read financial statements. Accordingly, we would therefore request that the nominations be accompanied by a copy of the nominee's curriculum vitae. Re-Election and Election of Directors In accordance with the provisions of the Company's Articles of Association, the Directors to retire by rotation at the AGM are: Mrs. Sola David-Borha, Mr. Ballama Manu; and Dr. Kunle Adedeji. The retiring Directors, being eligible, offer themselves for re-election. Also, in accordance with Section 278 (1) of the Companies and Allied Matters Act, 2020, SPECIAL NOTICE IS HEREBY GIVEN that Mr. Ballama Manu MFR, who attained the age of 70 years in June 2024, will be proposed for re-election as a Director at the Meeting. The appointment of Mr. Chukwuma Nwokocha as a Director of the Company will also be tabled at the AGM for formal approval. Notes Notice is herby given that the 14th Annual General Meeting of Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC will be held at the Jasmine Hall, Eko Hotel & Suites Plot 1415 Adetokunbo Ademola Street, Victoria Island, Lagos on Monday, 25 May 2026, at 10am to transact the following business: Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC RC 1018051 P06 P07 OVERVIEW Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC, Annual Report/Year ended 31 December 2025

Please tick 'l' in the appropriate box above before presenting this card for admission to the meeting * BEFORE POSTING THE ABOVE FORM OF PROXY, PLEASE TEAR OFF THIS PART AND RETAIN IT. Name of shareholders (in BLOCK LETTERS) Number of Shares Proxy Shareholder Signature of person attending Signature(s) of Shareholder(s) Name of Shareholder Proxy Shareholder Admission card RC 1018051 Account No: Shareholder's Name No. of Shares Account No: Shareholder's Name: No. of Shares: I, Mr/Mrs/Miss Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC RC 1018051 Profile of our new CEO Chuma Nwokocha is a seasoned banking executive and chartered accountant with over three decades of leadership experience across Africa. He was born on 11 November 1969 and resides in Lagos. He has held several Chief Executive and Board-level roles in leading financial institutions, driving strategic growth, governance, and operational excellence. He has honed his skills in leading high performing teams and his expertise spans retail and corporate banking, mergers and acquisitions, regulatory engagement, as well as regional expansion. He also possesses a strong track record of board governance, financial oversight, and strategic transformation across pan-African enterprises. Prior to his appointment as the Chief Executive of Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC, Chuma served as a Regional Managing Director at Access Bank, overseeing operations in Angola, Botswana, Mozambique, Zambia and green field expansion into Namibia. Before joining Access Bank PLC, Chuma served as Chief Executive of Standard Bank, South Africa (the Mozambican subsidiary of the Standard Bank Group), having also served in several leadership capacities across the bank including Head of Finance, Chief Operating Officer and Head of Personal & Business Banking between 2005 and 2022. He also spent five years in KPMG Mozambique as an Expatriate Manager between 2000 and 2005. A Fellow of the Institute of Chartered Accountants of Nigeria (ICAN), having trained with Coopers & Lybrand and subsequently working with PricewaterhouseCoopers, he is also an alumnus of Harvard, MIT Sloan, Oxford, and Henley Business School. He is a Non-Executive Director on the Board of Stanbic IBTC Pension Managers Limited. Chuma is a member of the following committees on the Board of Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC: • Board IT Committee • Board Risk Management Committee • Board Legal Committee Mr. Chuma Nwokocha P08 OVERVIEW P09 Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC, Annual Report/Year ended 31 December 2025

Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC ("Stanbic IBTC") was incorporated as a Public Limited Liability Company on 14 March 2012. Stanbic IBTC is the holding company for the entire Stanbic IBTC Group and its subsidiaries. The Company was listed on the Floor of The Nigerian Exchange Limited (NGX formerly The Nigerian Stock Exchange) on 23 November 2012, following the delisting of Stanbic IBTC's erstwhile holding company, Stanbic IBTC Bank Limited ("the Bank"), pursuant to its compliance with the CBN Regulation on Banking and Ancillary Matters No. 3 of 2010. The Bank on the other hand, was Investment Banking & Trust Company Limited ("IBTC"), a private limited liability company. IBTC was granted a merchant banking license in February 1989 and commenced operations on 01 March 1989. IBTC's merchant banking license was converted to a universal banking license in January 2002, pursuant to the universal banking guidelines of the Central Bank of Nigeria ("CBN"). In 2005, IBTC became a public company, and its shares were listed on NGX. In December 2005, IBTC merged with and changed its name to IBTC Chartered Bank PLC ("IBTC Chartered") on 25 January 2006. On 24 September 2007, IBTC Chartered merged with Stanbic Bank Nigeria Limited ("Stanbic Bank"), a wholly owned subsidiary of Stanbic Africa Holdings Limited ("SAHL"), which in turn is a wholly owned subsidiary of Standard Bank Group Limited of South Africa. As part of the transaction that resulted in the combination of IBTC Chartered and Stanbic Bank, SAHL acquired a majority equity stake (50.1%) in the enlarged bank, which was named Stanbic IBTC Bank Limited. On 01 November 2012, the Stanbic IBTC Group officially adopted a Holding Company ("Holdco") structure in compliance with CBN Regulation 3 of 2010, which requires banks to divest from non-core banking businesses or adopt a Holdco structure. Under the current structure, the subsidiaries are: Stanbic IBTC Bank Limited, Stanbic IBTC Pension Managers Limited, Stanbic IBTC Asset Management Limited, Stanbic IBTC Capital Limited, Stanbic IBTC Insurance Limited, Stanbic IBTC Stockbrokers Limited, Stanbic IBTC Ventures Limited, Stanbic IBTC Insurance Brokers Limited, Stanbic IBTC Trustees Limited, Zest Payments Limited and one indirect subsidiary, namely: Stanbic IBTC Nominees Limited. On 31 October 2023, Stanbic IBTC Bank ceased to be a public company and is now being referred to as Stanbic IBTC Bank Limited. The bank remains a wholly owned and no change of ownership, shareholding structure, or business objects of the company occurred as a result of its re-registration as a private company. The re-registration is to ensure the proper alignment of the bank's entity status and eliminate the existing brand confusion as many investors and members of the public hitherto referred to the bank as the listed entity and not Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC. Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC launched a Rights Issue in January 2025 to meet CBN's recapitalisation mandate, raising NGN148.7 billion and injecting NGN140 billion into its banking subsidiary, Stanbic IBTC Bank Limited. The exercise strengthened the Bank's capital position, increased SAHL's ownership to 68.46%, and ensured compliance with new regulatory capital requirements. Corporate profile Stanbic IBTC Nominees Limited 99.9% Stanbic IBTC Insurance Brokers Limited Stanbic IBTC Trustees Limited Stanbic IBTC Bank Limited Stanbic IBTC Ventures Limited Zest Payments Limited 75% 99.9% 99.9% 99.9% 99.9% Stanbic IBTC Capital Limited Stanbic IBTC Stockbrokers Limited Stanbic IBTC Pension Managers Limited Stanbic IBTC Asset Management Limited Stanbic IBTC Insurance Limited 99.9% 99.9% 88.24% 99.9% 99.9% List of shared services within the Stanbic IBTC Group The list below highlights the shared services within Stanbic IBTC Group. Corporate Functions: • Company Secretariat • Legal Services • Security • Real Estate Services • Procurement • Travel Desk • Information Technology The importance of shared services within the group are: • It helps to improve the group's structure by streamlining the existing shared services to allow for better operational efficiency. • Disclosure of the group's shared services on the website and the Annual report ensures that we are not in breach of CBN's financial holding company regulation. • It fosters standardisation, proper alignment of value benefits and cost allocation. • Reduction in the administrative overheads and increased efficiency. • It also improves the speed and accuracy of services across the group. Chairman's statement Distinguished shareholders, stakeholders, ladies and gentlemen, I am delighted to welcome you to the 2026 Annual General Meeting of Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC, convened to receive the audited financial statements of the Group and its subsidiaries year under review was defined by significant economic and regulatory reforms that reshaped Nigeria's financial landscape. Within this evolving environment, Stanbic IBTC remained firmly focused on executing its strategy, strengthening its balance sheet, and supporting clients across various sectors of the economy. This meeting allows us to present the outcomes of those efforts and reaffirm our commitment to sustainable, long-term value creation. This report is presented against the backdrop of one of the most significant economic reform cycles in Nigeria's recent history marked by far-reaching fiscal, monetary, and structural policy changes aimed at restoring macroeconomic stability and unlocking sustainable growth. 2025 financial year required resilience, discipline, and strategic clarity across the financial services sector. The reforms particularly in foreign exchange, subsidy rationalisation, and monetary tightening have reshaped the operating environment for businesses and households alike. While these adjustments introduced short-term volatility, they also created a more transparent, market-driven framework that supports long-term investment, productivity, and economic competitiveness. Within this evolving landscape, Stanbic IBTC remained firmly anchored in its purpose of driving Nigeria's growth while delivering sustainable value to its shareholders, clients, and communities. As we present the 2025 annual financial statements, I am pleased to reaffirm the unwavering commitment of Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC to sustain and build upon the strong financial achievements recorded in prior years. Our focus remains firmly on delivering consistent performance, safeguarding stakeholder value, and ensuring that the strategic objectives set by the Board continue to be met and surpassed. The Board and management, together with our highly committed workforce, remain united in our determination to deliver sustainable growth and long-term value. We are continuously focused on executing our strategy with precision, investing in our people and platforms, and responding proactively to the opportunities created by ongoing economic and regulatory reforms. Geopolitical trade tensions and global economic dynamics In 2025, the resurgence of protectionist trade policies notably renewed United States tariffs on major trading partners and strategic decoupling in U.S.-China economic relations materially influenced global trade patterns, supply chains, and inflationary pressures. U.S. tariff actions aimed at reducing trade imbalances led to significant export declines to the U.S. while prompting China to rebalance toward nonU.S. markets, including stronger ties with Europe, Southeast Asia, and Africa, resulting in a record trade surplus for China. These dynamics have led to softer global growth projections and evolving supply chain patterns that present both risks and opportunities for emerging markets. In response, Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC continues to closely track these developments, incorporating geopolitical considerations into its macroeconomic outlook and client advisories to support informed and strategic decision-making amid ongoing structural shifts. Our vibrant team According to official statistics, headline inflation eased to 15.15% in December 2025, the lowest in several years. Inflation down to 15.15% P30 P31 Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC, Annual Report/Year ended 31 December 2025

Chairman's statement (continued) The successful capital raise further demonstrates the Board's commitment to prudent financial stewardship. A substantial portion of the capital raised was injected into Stanbic IBTC Bank Limited, enabling the Bank to meet the Central Bank of Nigeria's new minimum capital requirement ahead of the 31 March 2026 deadline. This proactive approach underscores the Board's focus on regulatory compliance, balance sheet strength, and long-term financial stability, positioning the Group to continue supporting clients and the Nigerian economy with confidence. The Board recorded significant progress in complying with the Central Bank of Nigeria's Corporate Governance Guidelines for Financial Holding Companies in Nigeria. These improvements strengthened Board effectiveness, internal controls, risk governance, and management oversight across the Group. The Board continues to ensure that governance structures remain aligned with regulatory expectations and the best global practice, thereby reinforcing transparency, operational integrity, and stakeholder confidence. The Group made significant progress in the execution of its sustainability strategy, which remains firmly integrated into the Group's governance and decision-making processes. The Board exercises oversight over environmental, social, and governance priorities to ensure that capital allocation, Reflecting on Nigeria's economy In 2025, Nigeria's economy experienced a notable moderation in headline inflation, reflecting sustained policy efforts to anchor price stability. According to official statistics, headline inflation eased to 15.15% in December 2025, the lowest in several years, with food inflation declining to 10.84% and core inflation trending lower, highlighting improved price conditions across key consumption baskets. While inflation remains above levels typical of advanced economies, this sustained deceleration has alleviated cost-of-living pressures for households and contributed to a more predictable pricing environment for businesses. The Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) has maintained a dovish yet cautious monetary policy stance, retaining the Monetary Policy Rate (MPR) around 27% to balance inflation containment with support for economic activity. This approach reflects a calibrated shift from outright tightening toward gradual accommodation as inflationary pressures subside, consistent with the broader objective of stimulating output while safeguarding macro stability. In response to these evolving macroeconomic conditions, Stanbic IBTC has enhanced its strategic agility by integrating robust economic insights into credit risk frameworks, product pricing, and client advisory services risk management, and business strategy are aligned with long-term value creation Following the receipt of all required regulatory approvals, Mr. Chuma Nwokocha was appointed as the substantive Group Chief Executive with effect from 02 October 2025. This appointment represents a key governance milestone and provides the Group with strong leadership continuity, strategic clarity, and execution discipline. The Board remains confident in Mr. Nwokocha's ability to lead the organisation through a period of regulatory reform, capital strengthening, and sustainable growth while maintaining high standards of accountability and performance. Positive Impact Our commitment to sustainability is evidenced by the scale of capital we have deployed. For the year 2025, NGN459.2 billion was disbursed through sustainable finance instruments aligned to the Standard Bank Group Sustainable Finance Framework. This includes NGN391.7 billion in sustainable loans, representing 12.5% of our total loan book, and NGN94.9 billion in sustainability-linked notes. These funds are supporting renewable ener
[TRUNCATED]

operating_review.text:
Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC, Annual Report/Year ended 31 December 2025

Chairman's statement Distinguished shareholders, stakeholders, ladies and gentlemen, I am delighted to welcome you to the 2026 Annual General Meeting of Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC, convened to receive the audited financial statements of the Group and its subsidiaries year under review was defined by significant economic and regulatory reforms that reshaped Nigeria's financial landscape. Within this evolving environment, Stanbic IBTC remained firmly focused on executing its strategy, strengthening its balance sheet, and supporting clients across various sectors of the economy. This meeting allows us to present the outcomes of those efforts and reaffirm our commitment to sustainable, long-term value creation. This report is presented against the backdrop of one of the most significant economic reform cycles in Nigeria's recent history marked by far-reaching fiscal, monetary, and structural policy changes aimed at restoring macroeconomic stability and unlocking sustainable growth. 2025 financial year required resilience, discipline, and strategic clarity across the financial services sector. The reforms particularly in foreign exchange, subsidy rationalisation, and monetary tightening have reshaped the operating environment for businesses and households alike. While these adjustments introduced short-term volatility, they also created a more transparent, market-driven framework that supports long-term investment, productivity, and economic competitiveness. Within this evolving landscape, Stanbic IBTC remained firmly anchored in its purpose of driving Nigeria's growth while delivering sustainable value to its shareholders, clients, and communities. As we present the 2025 annual financial statements, I am pleased to reaffirm the unwavering commitment of Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC to sustain and build upon the strong financial achievements recorded in prior years. Our focus remains firmly on delivering consistent performance, safeguarding stakeholder value, and ensuring that the strategic objectives set by the Board continue to be met and surpassed. The Board and management, together with our highly committed workforce, remain united in our determination to deliver sustainable growth and long-term value. We are continuously focused on executing our strategy with precision, investing in our people and platforms, and responding proactively to the opportunities created by ongoing economic and regulatory reforms. Geopolitical trade tensions and global economic dynamics In 2025, the resurgence of protectionist trade policies notably renewed United States tariffs on major trading partners and strategic decoupling in U.S.-China economic relations materially influenced global trade patterns, supply chains, and inflationary pressures. U.S. tariff actions aimed at reducing trade imbalances led to significant export declines to the U.S. while prompting China to rebalance toward nonU.S. markets, including stronger ties with Europe, Southeast Asia, and Africa, resulting in a record trade surplus for China. These dynamics have led to softer global growth projections and evolving supply chain patterns that present both risks and opportunities for emerging markets. In response, Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC continues to closely track these developments, incorporating geopolitical considerations into its macroeconomic outlook and client advisories to support informed and strategic decision-making amid ongoing structural shifts. Our vibrant team According to official statistics, headline inflation eased to 15.15% in December 2025, the lowest in several years. Inflation down to 15.15% P30 P31 Stanbic IBTC Holdings PLC, Annual Report/Year ended 31 December 2025

Chairman's statement (continued) The successful capital raise further demonstrates the Board's commitment to prudent financial stewardship. A substantial portion of the capital raised was injected into Stanbic IBTC Bank Limited, enabling the Bank to meet the Central Bank of Nigeria's new minimum capital requirement ahead of the 31 March 2026 deadline. This proactive approach underscores the Board's focus on regulatory compliance, balance sheet strength, and long-term financial stability, positioning the Group to continue supporting clients and the Nigerian economy with confidence. The Board recorded significant progress in complying with the Central Bank of Nigeria's Corporate Governance Guidelines for Financial Holding Companies in Nigeria. These improvements strengthened Board effectiveness, internal controls, risk
